trajectory_summary: The call is structurally complete — address confirmed, utility captured, kWh read, appointment booked — but emotionally flat from start to finish. Vernon ran a clean data-collection pass; he did not build a reason for Robert to care about Monday's appointment. The booking is thin: Robert's 'it should work' and 'I'll put it out' language signals accommodation, not investment.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is likely 'booked' — that is technically accurate but overstates the quality of the outcome. This is a thin booking: emotional investment was not built, the bill pain was never flipped into motivation, and the closer will need to establish urgency from scratch. The appointment is at no-show risk not because Robert is hostile but because he has no anchored reason to prioritize it.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: Oh, really? Okay. That makes sense. Yeah. It makes sense. Thank you for kinda clarifying, like, the higher and lower ones too. Makes it easier for us.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Robert gave Vernon a $500 winter peak and a self-calculated $3,500–$4,000 annual figure — the highest-leverage emotional material on this call — and Vernon filed both as data points useful to Ion. The schema's capture-and-flip pattern (coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring) was available twice and unused twice. This is the single reason the booking is thin: Robert has no emotional reason to show up Monday. The manager should use this moment to walk Vernon through the flip mechanic specifically — not as a technique lecture, but by replaying this exact exchange and asking 'what does Robert feel after your response?' The answer is: nothing. That's the gap.

key_moments.quote: Hey. Hey, Robert. This is Vernon with Ion. I was, my coworker, Meg, had just mentioned to me that you received a call from us.
key_moments.stewart_read: Vernon uses a warm-transfer framing ('my coworker Meg mentioned you') that matches neither schema intro variant. The standard intro anchors legitimacy via the customer's own form submission; the Tesla intro anchors via the certified-installer relationship. This intro anchors on an internal referral the customer has no context for, which neither establishes legitimacy nor references the customer's own action. The hard-required intro beat is present in form but misses its legitimacy function.

key_moments.quote: Well, good point. In the wintertime, it has gone to $500 a month. I just got one to this past month with a little over 200, but I would guess k. At least 300 a month.
key_moments.stewart_read: Robert volunteers a $500 winter peak and a $300 average — meaningful bill pain delivered unprompted. Vernon's response ('Oh, really? Okay. That makes sense') is administrative acknowledgment. The schema's capture-and-flip pattern requires the bill to be turned into emotional alignment against the utility. The flip was available: the $500 winter number is the lever. It was not pulled.

Robert calculated his annual utility spend for tax purposes — $3,500–$4,000. This is not a casual estimate; it is a number he owns and has thought about. That level of financial awareness makes him a higher-quality lead: he knows his pain precisely, which means the closer can anchor on a real number rather than an estimate. Vernon's response ('Thank you for kinda clarifying... Makes it easier for us') frames this as useful to Ion, not meaningful to Robert. The enthusiasm signal — a customer who tracks his bills for tax purposes is a customer who will respond to a concrete savings conversation — was walked past entirely.

key_moments.quote: Well, actually, the house is in a partnership with my sister and me, but I'm the general partner, the one who runs everything.
key_moments.stewart_read: Robert discloses that the property is co-owned with his sister, who is on the title. Under protocols.spouse_decision and protocols.adult_child_decision_maker, a co-title-holder must be included in the appointment. Vernon's response ('Yeah. You're kind of the boss there') accepts Robert's self-designation as sole decision-maker and moves on. Robert may well be the functional decision-maker, but the sister's title interest is a structural vulnerability: if she has any role in a purchase decision, the closer walks into an appointment that can be blocked by an absent party.
